Chapter 1: Introduction

An example chapter page using the technical document template.

Document Information

Version
0.1.0
Last updated
2026-04-25
Author
Atsushi Suzuki

Overview

This page can run Python in the browser with Pyodide. A server-side Python environment is not required, but the first runtime load can take some time because Pyodide is downloaded on demand.

Use a supplemental note (aside.callout-success) for helpful context, prerequisites, successful states, and recommendations. Example: This template is designed to remain readable on narrow screens.
Use a warning note (aside.callout-warning) for constraints, environment-specific behavior, and exceptions readers should know in advance. Example: Python execution, Mermaid, MathJax, and Prism depend on JavaScript; the chapter contents and navigation are generated at build time.

Template Usage

This section is a minimal editing guide for creating a new HTML document from the template. It shows the main writing patterns so both human editors and generation models can extend the document consistently. For an interactive code example, see .

Use <section id="..." data-toc data-toc-title="..."> for top-level sections that should appear in the table of contents. For optional subsection entries, add id, data-toc, and data-toc-level="3" to the heading.

Common Components

  • Supplemental notes and recommendations: <aside class="callout callout-success">
  • Warnings and constraints: <aside class="callout callout-warning">
  • Standard body card: <div class="card">
  • Muted card: <div class="card card-muted">
  • Compact card: <div class="card card-compact">
  • Code display: <pre class="code-block language-python"><code class="language-python">
  • Math: use \(...\) for inline math and \[...\] for display math
  • Mermaid diagrams: write the diagram definition inside <div class="mermaid">.
The Python runner, Mermaid, MathJax, Prism, and copy buttons are JavaScript enhancements. The sidebar contents, heading references, and previous/next chapter navigation are generated at build time. Keep important explanations in normal HTML body text so the document remains readable if JavaScript is unavailable.

Math Examples

These examples show inline and display math rendered by MathJax. When creating a new document, copy this section and replace the content while preserving the display patterns.

Inline Math

Use \(...\) for short formulas inside a paragraph. For example, a normal distribution can be written as \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)\) using mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^2\). The standardized value is \(z = (x - \mu) / \sigma\).

Display Math

Use \[...\] for standalone formulas, optionally wrapped in div.math-block. The density definition in and the moment identities in are numbered examples.

\[ f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \]
\[ \mathbb{E}[X] = \mu, \qquad \mathrm{Var}(X) = \sigma^2, \qquad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,dx = 1 \]

Python Code Display and Execution

Use static code blocks for code that readers only need to inspect, and use the browser runner for code readers should edit and execute. This keeps explanatory snippets separate from interactive examples.

Python Code Block

For code that does not need to run in the browser, combine pre.code-block with code.language-python. Place div.code-caption above the block to show the language name or file name.

python
def summarize_scores(scores: list[int]) -> dict[str, float]:
    """Returns simple summary statistics for a score list."""
    total = sum(scores)
    count = len(scores)

    return {
        "count": count,
        "average": total / count,
        "maximum": max(scores),
    }


scores = [82, 90, 76, 88]
summary = summarize_scores(scores)
print(summary)

Executable Code Example

Use the runner source block for code that readers should edit and execute. The build script expands div[data-python-runner] into the full browser runner UI.

The Python runner loads Pyodide from a CDN only after the reader presses Load Python Runtime. Pyodide is loaded with importScripts() inside a Web Worker, so normal script-tag SRI with integrity cannot be used there. For documents that require strict supply-chain control, host a reviewed Pyodide distribution locally.

Try changing the input sizes and run the code.

from time import perf_counter


def linear_search_with_count(values: list[int], target: int) -> tuple[int, int]:
    """Finds the target by scanning values and counts comparisons.

    Args:
        values: Sorted integer values.
        target: Value to find.

    Returns:
        A pair of the target index and the number of comparisons.
    """
    comparisons = 0

    for index, value in enumerate(values):
        comparisons += 1
        if value == target:
            return index, comparisons

    return -1, comparisons


def binary_search_with_count(values: list[int], target: int) -> tuple[int, int]:
    """Finds the target using binary search and counts comparisons.

    Args:
        values: Sorted integer values.
        target: Value to find.

    Returns:
        A pair of the target index and the number of comparisons.
    """
    left = 0
    right = len(values) - 1
    comparisons = 0

    while left <= right:
        middle = (left + right) // 2
        comparisons += 1

        if values[middle] == target:
            return middle, comparisons

        if values[middle] < target:
            left = middle + 1
        else:
            right = middle - 1

    return -1, comparisons


def measure_once(n: int, repeats: int) -> tuple[int, int, float, float]:
    """Measures comparison counts and average execution times.

    Args:
        n: Number of elements.
        repeats: Number of repeated searches.

    Returns:
        Linear-search comparisons, binary-search comparisons, average linear-search
        time, and average binary-search time.
    """
    values = list(range(n))
    target = n - 1

    _, linear_comparisons = linear_search_with_count(values, target)
    _, binary_comparisons = binary_search_with_count(values, target)

    start = perf_counter()
    for _ in range(repeats):
        linear_search_with_count(values, target)
    linear_ms = (perf_counter() - start) * 1000 / repeats

    start = perf_counter()
    for _ in range(repeats):
        binary_search_with_count(values, target)
    binary_ms = (perf_counter() - start) * 1000 / repeats

    return linear_comparisons, binary_comparisons, linear_ms, binary_ms


def main() -> None:
    """Runs a simple growth-rate experiment."""
    sizes = [1_000, 10_000, 100_000, 500_000, 1_000_000]
    repeats_by_size = {
        1_000: 200,
        10_000: 100,
        100_000: 20,
        500_000: 5,
        1_000_000: 3,
    }

    print("n          linear_cmp   binary_cmp   cmp_ratio   linear_ms   binary_ms")
    print("-" * 78)

    for n in sizes:
        linear_cmp, binary_cmp, linear_ms, binary_ms = measure_once(n, repeats_by_size[n])
        ratio = linear_cmp / binary_cmp if binary_cmp > 0 else float("inf")
        print(
            f"{n:>9}  "
            f"{linear_cmp:>10}  "
            f"{binary_cmp:>10}  "
            f"{ratio:>9.1f}x  "
            f"{linear_ms:>9.4f}  "
            f"{binary_ms:>9.4f}"
        )

    print()
    print("Interpretation:")
    print("- Comparison counts are the cleanest signal here.")
    print("- Linear search comparisons grow almost directly with n.")
    print("- Binary search comparisons grow slowly because the search range is halved.")
    print("- Timing numbers are useful but noisier than comparison counts.")


main()

Other Languages

Code in other languages can use the same visual style by switching the Prism language class. This template loads the PowerShell and SQL Prism components by default.

PowerShell Code Block

powershell
Get-ChildItem -Path . -Filter "*.html" |
    Select-Object Name, Length, LastWriteTime |
    Sort-Object LastWriteTime -Descending

SQL Code Block

sql
SELECT
    document_id,
    title,
    updated_at
FROM documents
WHERE status = "published"
ORDER BY updated_at DESC;
To add another language such as cmd, bash, or JSON, load the corresponding Prism language component and change the language-... class to match. If you add files from a CDN, also add SRI metadata for those files.

Mermaid Diagrams

Mermaid renders flowcharts and structural diagrams from text definitions. You can update a diagram by editing text in the HTML instead of recreating an image. Mermaid also supports sequence diagrams, class diagrams, state diagrams, and more. The rendered result is referenced as .

mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Define the document goal] --> B[Plan the section structure]
    B --> C[Write the body text]
    B --> D[Create exercises or code examples]
    C --> E[Add diagrams and math]
    D --> F[Check executable examples]
    E --> G[Review the rendered document]
    F --> G

Operational Notes

Interpreting Runtime Results

Browser execution time is affected by device performance, battery state, browser optimization, and other running processes. Treat timing output as a rough signal for behavior and trends, not as a strict benchmark.

Progressive Reading Design

Body text, links, and code content remain readable as normal HTML. Sidebar contents, heading references, and chapter navigation are present in the generated HTML. Python execution, Mermaid, MathJax, Prism, and copy buttons are convenience enhancements added by JavaScript.

Changelog

The release history is shown in .
